scenario: Tessera Insurance correlates an external payment notification to a single waiting policy instance among 8000 policies in Camunda 8.8. The architect must avoid broadcasting the notification to every active instance, so the integration must use the 1:1 sender-to-recipient event category.
question: Which event type delivers a 1:1 correlation from one publisher to one waiting process instance?

options.text: A message event delivers a 1:1 correlation, routing the published message to the single waiting instance that matches the message name and correlation key value
A signal event delivers a 1:1 correlation, routing the broadcast to the single instance whose name matches the published signal value at the throw site of the event
A message event delivers a 1:N correlation, fanning the published message out to every waiting instance whose start event references the same configured message name
A signal event delivers a 1:1 correlation by name match only, ignoring any correlation key value supplied alongside the published signal payload from the client

a.text: Correct. The documentation states that message events are used if a single process instance needs to wait for a message, forming a 1:1 sender-to-recipient relationship that cannot have more than one recipient.

b.text: Incorrect. Signal events form a 1:N relationship in the documentation; a thrown signal triggers every waiting catch in matching processes, which is the opposite of a 1:1 correlation.

c.text: Incorrect. Messages are described as 1:1 in the documentation, and the message has only one recipient; the 1:N fan-out behavior belongs to signal events instead.

d.text: Incorrect. Signals route by name to every matching listener in a 1:N relationship; they are not described as a 1:1 correlation channel for a single waiting instance.

explanation: The documentation contrasts messages with signals: message events form a single-sender to single-recipient (1:1) relationship, while signal events form a single-sender to several-recipient (1:N) relationship.
